title: Public Housing vs. Section 8 (Housing Choice Voucher) in Cleveland
metaTitle: Public Housing vs Section 8 in Cleveland, Explained
metaDescription: Public housing vs Section 8 in Cleveland: how CMHA's programs differ, who administers each, and how a voucher works at a private rental. Book a showing.
secondaryKeywords: difference between public housing and section 8
cmha housing choice voucher vs public housing
answerBox: Public housing is a government-owned unit that a housing authority operates directly, while a Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher is a portable rent subsidy a household uses to rent from a private landlord — like the homes Rent Finder Cleveland works with. CMHA, the Cuyahoga Metropolitan Housing Authority, administers both programs, but they work very differently for a renter.

body: Public housing refers to rental units that a local public housing authority — in Cuyahoga County, that's CMHA — owns and operates directly, with HUD funding the program. A household applies to the housing authority itself, and if selected, rents a specific unit that the authority (not a private landlord) manages. Public housing developments are a fixed, limited supply tied to specific buildings and addresses that the authority controls.

sections.h2: What is a Housing Choice Voucher (Section 8)?
body: A Housing Choice Voucher works differently: instead of living in a unit the housing authority owns, a voucher holder finds and rents a unit on the open private market — from any landlord willing to accept the voucher — and the housing authority pays a portion of the rent directly to that landlord each month. CMHA accepts preliminary Housing Choice Voucher applications online year-round with no closing date, and selects applicants through periodic random "mini-lotteries" rather than strict first-come, first-served order. Before a voucher can be used on a given unit, that unit must pass a HUD housing-quality inspection — a standard HUD is currently transitioning from the older HQS framework to NSPIRE, with a final deadline of February 1, 2027 for the [Housing Choice Voucher program](https://www.hud.gov/helping-americans/housing-choice-vouchers).
The practical difference for a renter comes down to who owns the unit and how much choice you have over where you live.

rows: Any participating private rental you choose
The housing authority, then a private landlord
Can generally be used across jurisdictions ("porting")

sections.h2: Income limits apply to either program
body: Whether a household is applying for public housing or a Housing Choice Voucher, HUD income limits determine eligibility, and CMHA uses the same underlying limits to screen applicants for both programs. As of CMHA's 2025 figures, a one-person household generally must earn no more than about $34,800 a year, rising to roughly $39,800 for two people, $49,700 for four, and $65,650 for eight — these limits are updated periodically, so always check CMHA's current chart before applying rather than relying on last year's numbers.

Application timelines can also differ in practice: because public housing units are limited to whatever a given development has available, and vouchers can be used at any of the many private rentals willing to accept one, a voucher holder often has a wider immediate pool of potential homes to search once selected from the waitlist, even though both programs draw from the same overall preliminary application and lottery process at CMHA.

There's also a middle category worth knowing about: a project-based voucher is attached to a specific building rather than portable to any landlord a household chooses, which makes it behave more like public housing in that sense, even though it's still a HUD voucher program administered by the housing authority. See our [project-based voucher vs. Housing Choice Voucher guide](/cleveland-rentals/section-8/project-based-voucher-vs-housing-choice-voucher/) for how that distinction plays out for renters.

sections.h2: Renting one of our homes with a Housing Choice Voucher
body: Rent Finder Cleveland is a local rental team, not a housing authority — we don't operate public housing developments. What we do work with is 90+ rental homes across the greater Cleveland area, and every single one accepts Housing Choice Vouchers and is kept HUD-inspection-ready. Our inventory is concentrated in Cleveland's Slavic Village, Collinwood, Glenville, Fairfax/Central, Hough, and Buckeye-Shaker neighborhoods, with additional homes on the west side and in Akron, Lorain, and Elyria. If you already hold a Housing Choice Voucher, that's exactly the kind of private-market rental it's designed to work with. Read our full [Cleveland Section 8 guide](/section-8-housing-cleveland-oh/) for the local application process, or [book a free showing](/p/book-showing) to see current availability.

faq.q: What's the main difference between public housing and Section 8?
faq.a: Public housing is a government-owned unit operated directly by a housing authority, while a Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher is a subsidy a household uses to rent from a private landlord of their choosing. Public housing ties you to a specific authority-owned building; a voucher lets you search the private rental market.

faq.q: Does CMHA run both public housing and Housing Choice Vouchers in Cuyahoga County?
faq.a: Yes. CMHA, the Cuyahoga Metropolitan Housing Authority, administers both types of federally funded housing assistance for the county, though they work differently: public housing units it owns directly, versus vouchers that subsidize rent at private-market rentals.

faq.q: Can I use a Housing Choice Voucher at a private landlord's rental home?
faq.a: Yes — that's exactly how the program is designed to work. A Housing Choice Voucher lets a household rent from any private landlord willing to accept it, as long as the unit passes a HUD housing-quality inspection first.

faq.q: What is a project-based voucher and how is it different?
faq.a: A project-based voucher is attached to a specific building rather than portable to any landlord you choose, which makes it function more like public housing even though HUD and the local housing authority still administer it as a voucher program.

faq.q: Does Rent Finder Cleveland works with public housing units?
faq.a: No. We're a local rental team, not a housing authority, so we don't operate public housing developments. Every one of the 90+ homes we work with is a private-market rental that accepts Housing Choice Vouchers and is HUD-inspection-ready.
